title: Omega-3 Fatty Acid Deficiency Risk Assessment
description: Check your omega-3 deficiency risk with our free quiz. Evaluate symptoms like dry skin, joint pain, brain fog, and mood changes in this evidence-based assessment.
scientificBasis: Based on the Omega-3 Index methodology (Harris & Von Schacky, 2004, Preventive Medicine) and clinical symptoms of essential fatty acid deficiency. Optimal Omega-3 Index is 8–12%; deficiency-associated symptoms appear below 4%.
seoIntro:
Omega-3 fatty acids — particularly EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) and DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) — are essential fats that your body cannot produce on its own. They play critical roles in brain function, heart health, inflammation regulation, joint mobility, skin hydration, and mood stability.

Despite their importance, omega-3 deficiency is one of the most common nutritional gaps in Western diets. The shift toward processed foods high in omega-6 fatty acids (vegetable oils, fried foods, packaged snacks) has created an omega-6 to omega-3 ratio that is dramatically skewed — often 15:1 or higher, versus the ideal ratio of roughly 2:1.

Symptoms of omega-3 insufficiency can be widespread: dry, flaky, or itchy skin; dry eyes; joint stiffness and pain; mood instability or depression; difficulty concentrating; brittle nails; and frequent inflammation. Because these symptoms develop gradually and overlap with other conditions, omega-3 deficiency is frequently overlooked.

This self-assessment evaluates your dietary intake of omega-3-rich foods, supplementation habits, and symptoms against the Omega-3 Index framework used in cardiovascular and nutritional research. If your results suggest concern, an Omega-3 Index blood test can measure your actual EPA and DHA levels in red blood cell membranes.

questions.text: How often do you eat fatty fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines, herring)?
Do you take an omega-3 supplement (fish oil, algae oil)?
Do you experience dry, flaky, or itchy skin?

options.label: Chronically — I use eye drops regularly

questions.text: How would you describe your mood stability?
Do you experience joint stiffness or pain?
How would you rate your ability to concentrate and focus?
Do you consume walnuts, flaxseeds, chia seeds, or hemp seeds regularly?
Do you eat a lot of processed foods high in omega-6 (fried foods, vegetable oils, packaged snacks)?

results.title: Low Risk — Omega-3 Intake Likely Adequate
results.description: Your omega-3 intake and symptoms suggest adequate levels. Continue your current diet rich in fatty fish and omega-3 sources.
recommendations: Continue eating fatty fish 2+ times per week
Maintain current omega-3 supplementation if applicable

results.title: Mild Risk — Suboptimal Omega-3 Status Possible
results.description: Some indicators of suboptimal omega-3 status. Consider increasing fatty fish or supplementation.
recommendations: Increase fatty fish intake to 2+ servings per week
Add walnuts, flaxseeds, and chia seeds to your diet
Consider a fish oil or algae oil supplement (1000–2000 mg EPA+DHA daily)
Reduce processed food and omega-6-heavy oils

results.title: Moderate Risk — Possible Omega-3 Insufficiency
results.description: Multiple signs of possible omega-3 insufficiency. An Omega-3 Index blood test can confirm.
recommendations: Consider an Omega-3 Index blood test
Start a high-quality fish oil or algae oil supplement
Reduce omega-6 intake from processed and fried foods

results.title: High Risk — Likely Omega-3 Deficiency
results.description: Strong deficiency profile. Supplement with high-quality fish oil or algae oil and consult a provider.
recommendations: Start an omega-3 supplement immediately (2000+ mg EPA+DHA daily)
Request an Omega-3 Index blood test
Overhaul your diet to include more omega-3-rich foods
Consult a healthcare provider or dietitian for a personalized plan

faq.question: What is the Omega-3 Index?
faq.answer: The Omega-3 Index measures the percentage of EPA and DHA in your red blood cell membranes. An index of 8–12% is optimal and associated with lowest cardiovascular risk. Below 4% is considered deficient. It's a simple blood test available through most labs.

faq.question: Can I get enough omega-3 from plant sources alone?
faq.answer: Plant sources like flaxseeds, chia seeds, and walnuts provide ALA (alpha-linolenic acid), which your body must convert to EPA and DHA. However, this conversion rate is very low (typically under 5%). For optimal EPA/DHA levels, fatty fish or algae-based supplements are recommended.

faq.question: How much omega-3 do I need daily?
faq.answer: Most health organizations recommend at least 250–500 mg of combined EPA and DHA daily for general health. For people with heart disease risk or inflammatory conditions, 1000–2000 mg or more may be recommended. Always choose a supplement tested for purity.

sources: Harris, W.S. & Von Schacky, C. (2004). The Omega-3 Index: a new risk factor for death from coronary heart disease? Preventive Medicine, 39(1), 212-220.
Swanson, D. et al. (2012). Omega-3 fatty acids EPA and DHA: health benefits throughout life. Advances in Nutrition, 3(1), 1-7.
National Institutes of Health. Office of Dietary Supplements — Omega-3 Fatty Acids Fact Sheet.
